Review: Push Nine One V2 Inverted Fork - Plush & Pricey

Nov 18, 2025
by Mike Kazimer  
Share
Tweet
You must login to Pinkbike.
Don't have an account? Sign up

Join Pinkbike  Login
450 Comments
Push Nine One V2
0 seconds of 0 secondsVolume 0%

13:54
 

Just like baggy jeans and Nirvana t-shirts, upside down forks are experiencing something of a resurgence. Fox, Intend, and Push have all released enduro-oriented options, and Cane Creek has teased a prototype USD fork of their own. Is it a sign of things to come, or will the USD concept remain something of a fringe option in a sea of right-side-up forks? I’m leaning towards the latter, but time will tell.

Push first revealed the Nine One at Sea Otter 2023, but it took some time before it finally hit the market. Once it did, the Colorado-based company didn’t waste any time before rolling out version 2.0, which adds on some clever features not found on the initial version.
Push Nine One V2 Details

• Coil sprung
• Travel: 140-170 mm (internally adjustable)
• 15 x 110mm thru-axle
• Adjustments: high- & low-speed compression, rebound, Sub Chamber, hydraulic bottom out
• 29" or 27.5" wheel
• Torque Cap compatible
• Weight: 2,950 grams w/ axle
• MSRP: $2,250 USD
• pushindustries.com


Details

Let’s start with the basics. The Nine One is coil sprung, with external rebound, high and low-speed compression adjustments. There’s also adjustable hydraulic bottom out, and a feature Push calls the Sub Chamber, which can be toggled on or off to add end-stroke progression.

HBO

The hydraulic bottom kicks in for the last 70mm of travel. It's almost like a shock within a shock - it uses a monotube layout with its own main piston, plus an adjustable internal floating piston (IFP) that can be inflated up to 40 psi. As the chart below illustrates, thanks to its speed sensitive design it provides much more bottom-out resistance at higher velocities than the ABS (air bump stop) feature found on version 1.0 of this fork.


Sub Chamber

The Sub Chamber is another very clever feature - it uses the ambient air trapped in the fork to create additional mid-stroke support. Push uses the following analogy to describe how it functions: "Take a plastic water bottle and empty the water from the bottle. Now squeeze the bottle. With the cap removed the bottle is very easy to crush in your hand. Now, expand the bottle, tighten down the cap, and squeeze the bottle again. With the cap installed ambient air has been trapped inside the bottle causing resistance or creating a basic air spring." That's what the Sub Chamber does - when it's closed off the air is trapped in the fork, adding that extra support.


The Nine One's upper tubes measure 44mm in diameter, and the stanchions are 36mm. The Nine One uses a 15mm x 110mm thru-axle, and is compatible with Torque Cap hubs (that fact will be more pertinent later on in this review). The axle is secured by four pinch bolts.

The amount of travel can be set between 140 - 170mm in 10mm increments, a procedure that can be performed at home without the need for any additional parts. It does require basic mechanical skills, and it’s not the quickest task, but still, it’s nice not needing to shell out any extra dollars for another spring assembly.

The two-position Sub Chamber dial.

Low-speed compression and high-speed compression are adjusted on the right side, with nice and distinct clicks with each turn.

Price & Weight

On my scale the Nine One V2 weighed in at 2950 grams with the axle installed - that’s 255 grams heavier than a Fox Podium, and it’s also heavier than a RockShox Boxxer or a Fox 40, which are dual-crown DH forks.

As for the price, that’s on the higher side of things too - it’ll set you back $2,250 USD, and you’ll likely want to shell out another $35 for the fender.

Push Nine One V2: 2950 grams | $2,250 USD. 140 - 170mm travel
Fox Podium: 2695g | $2,000 USD. 150-170mm travel.
Fox 38: 2381g | $1,249 USD. 150-180mm travel.
Intend Flash: 2350g | $2,263 USD. 160-190mm travel.
RockShox Zeb: 2324g | $1,159 USD. 150-190mm travel.


Setup

I’ve had the Nine One V2 in my test rotation for the last four months, and during that time it’s been installed on a Trek Fuel LX, a Specialized Levo 4, and a Yeti MTe. To me, those represent the trio of bike styles that I envision potential customers installing this fork on - a burly enduro bike, a full power eMTB, and a mid-power eMTB.

Testing began in the Whistler Bike Park during Crankworx, where the fork was subjected to plenty of deep braking bumps and harsh compressions, and continued on the trails surrounding Bellingham, Washington.

The fork arrives with a handy little setup guide, with a section that has the base settings written down, and the signatures of the employees that assembled and inspected the fork. There are a total of seven spring weights available, from 30 to 60 lb/in in 5 pound increments. For my 160 lb weight I went with the 45 lb spring, and started with the low and high speed compression dials at 15 clicks out from closed, and the rebound at 12 clicks out. The IFP for the hydraulic bottom out feature was inflated to 30 psi (40 psi is the max).


On the Trail

Damping

The extra-sensitive initial stroke is the trait that first sticks out, no matter whether you’re climbing or descending. Now, the climbing performance of a fork isn’t usually a big topic of conversation, but the ease that the Nine One goes into its stroke is worth a few extra lines of electronic ink.

Some forks are plush enough to make you want to actively run into things just to see what’ll happen, and the Nine One falls into that category. Small undulations in the trail are erased, and larger obstacles, whether that’s an anaconda-sized root or big jumble of chunky rocks, are easily dispatched with a ker-squish, ker-squish (that’s the sound of the damper doing its thing - the Nine One isn’t the quietest fork).

That smoothness (and the audible damper noise) carries over onto the descents - straightline plowing is when the fork feels the best. I experimented with both Sub Chamber settings, and found that for the most part I preferred to have it on, due to the extra support it provides deeper in the fork’s travel. For maximum bump-suckability, turning it off gives the fork the fully linear, classic coil spring feeling, right up until the last portion of the travel when the hydraulic bottom out is activated to prevent any clunks or clangs.

I ended up pretty much in the middle of the compression settings, and I still had 6 more clicks to go before being fully open on rebound, which means there should be a good range of options for riders to play with in order to achieve their ideal setup.


Stiffness

Rough, twisty trails, the type that Whistler’s Creekside zone is full of (Miss Fire, Ride Don’t Slide, and BC’s Trail for example) start to bring the Nine One’s main weakness to light - the lack of stiffness in those slower speed, heavy front end movement scenarios. At times it felt like the wheel wanted to wander, as if it was grabbing at the edges of the trail when all I wanted to do was keep going forward.

After my first few rides where the amount of front end flex felt unexpectedly high, even for a USD fork, I contacted Darren Murphy, the owner of Push, to see if he had any tips. His suggestion? A different hub. It turns out that some hubs are better than others at resisting the independent leg movement that comes with an upside-down fork design; in this instance he suggested Industry Nine’s Hydra hub, or at the very least using Torque Caps, the oversized end caps that are available for some hubs. I happened to have a Forge & Bond (RIP) wheel with an I9 hub and Torque Caps, so I made the swap and started putting in the miles again.

That hub change made a more noticeable difference than I expected - the amount of deflection was reduced, and the stiffness was closer to an acceptable level, although there were still scenarios where the handling was less precise than I wanted. The fork felt very good when the trails were greasy, instances when the best tactic is to avoid overly-aggressive corrections, but when things were drier the extra front-end movement was more noticeable - even with a different hub the Nine One didn't have the level of precision I was looking for.


Weight

The high weight of the Nine One is noticeable, especially on a bike without a motor. On the Trek, it made the front wheel feel like it wanted to stay glued to the ground, and the bike as a whole felt less eager to get airborne. Granted, it was already pushing 40 pounds before installing the Push, but the extra heft of the fork definitely didn’t help. The plushness, which is great for traction, adds to that glued-down sensation too.

On the e-bike, especially the Levo, the weight was a little less noticeable, since that bike is already pretty heavy as it is, but it's not doing it any favors in the maneuverability department.

Push Nine One V2

Fox Podium

How Does it Compare?

The most obvious contender to pit against the Nine One V2 is the Fox Podium. Both forks are inverted, they’re both expensive ($2,250 for the Push and $2,000 for the Podium), and neither one is particularly light (2950 grams for the Push, and 2695 for the Podium). The Podium is air sprung, though, which does save some weight, and it uses a 20mm steel thru-axle, which helps in the stiffness department.

Out on the trail, the Nine One wins in the initial sensitivity department. The Podium is no slouch, and it’s extremely smooth, but in this air vs coil battle the Nine One snags the plushness point. Push also takes the point when it comes to bottom out resistance. Yes, the Podium can be tuned with volume spacers to increase the amount of ramp up, but it’s much easier to achieve a smooth end-stroke ramp up on the Nine One thanks to the Sub Chamber and the hydraulic bottom out features.

The Podium pulls ahead when it comes to stiffness and precision. It’s not as torsionally stiff as a 38 (and it’s not claimed to be), but I found the Podium’s handling to be more predictable than the Nine One, especially in those aforementioned steep, rough, slower speed twisty trails. With the Nine One, there were times when it was a little hard to discern what the front wheel was doing. It may seem odd to want more feedback from a fork, but just like it’d be hard to climb a steep sport route while wearing thick wool socks, it can be harder to navigate a trail when the sensations reaching your hands are extra-muted.

Dario DiGiulio also spent time on both forks, and his findings mirrored mine - he found the chassis flex to be the biggest differentiator between the two. He’s 185 pounds vs my 160 pound weight, and for him the flex in the Nine One was even more excessive in tight corners and hard compressions. His takeaway? "Though the top-end feel of the fork is very impressive, I can’t think of any real-world applications where I’d rather ride this fork than something like a 38, Zeb, or the Podium."

Personally, I’m not totally sold on either upside-down option. I’d pick the Podium over the Nine One, since it’s stiffer and quieter, but I’d pick the 38 over either one - I prefer the precision and lighter weight of that fork, even if it’s not quite as plush off the top. It’s also almost half the cost, and to me the performance difference isn’t worth an extra $1,000, no matter how cool an upside down fork looks.




Pros

+ Very smooth and plush thanks to coil spring and USD design
+ Sub Chamber feature is very effective
+ Optional fender works and actually looks decent


Cons

- Noticeable flex in certain scenarios
- Heavy
- Expensive




Pinkbike's Take
I’ll always root for the underdog, especially when a small company is trying to go up against the bike industry behemoths. This time, though, David’s flying rocks aren't enough to take down Goliath. The Nine One is as plush as it gets, and it’ll absolutely plow through the rough stuff, at least if you’re going in a straight line, but the lack of stiffness holds it back on slower speed, tighter trails, especially ones that require heavy braking.

That chassis flex is the main sticking point - the 15mm thru-axle has its work cut out for it, and there’s a distinct lack of front end precision in certain scenarios. There’s also the extra weight to contend with, and the sky high price. For riders that are enamored with the looks and traction that the Nine One delivers those points can be overlooked, but for most riders going with a right-side-up, less expensive option will be the best bet. — Mike Kazimer


Posted In:

Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

Kilian1b15Gt (Nov 19, 2025 at 2:10)
 the problem is the frame. not a lot frames are approved for dual crown forks since they put more stress on the frame in case of a crash. The rubber bump stops of dual crown forks usually hit rather weak parts of the frame since those arent particularly stressed. The fork also transmits more energy into the frame in a head on crash

  • 50
briain (Nov 19, 2025 at 4:40)
 @Mtbdialed: Like the rest of components everything is getting heavier. The 3mm boxxer weighed less than a ZEB or 38. It seems big enduro bikes would be better served with DC crown forks. ALthough my ebike with its silly ZS66 headtube doesn´t have enough steering lock for a DC fork. Maybe another standard that needs to die
  • 40
cougar797 (Nov 19, 2025 at 7:28)
 I did a new enduro / FR build real recently. I really looked at this fork and the podium hard. For the prices, weight, performance, reported reliability, I ended up on a carbon dorado w/ 20mm axle lowered to 180mm at the same A2C as a 170mm single crown. I considered the intend too but $$$ and support parts was scarier there. I love my setup. I really think USD dual crown is the ultimate solution. Edit: that new boxxer at 180mm would be sick too by the sounds of it.
  • 20
cougar797 (Nov 19, 2025 at 7:29)
 @letsgethurt: I just did this. See my post. Its 100% a wonderful solution.
  • 10
cougar797 (Nov 19, 2025 at 7:32)
 @Kilian1b15Gt: I think the extra stress thing is kind of a myth. Stress at heatube is stress at headtube. What manufactures worry about most is excessive length and headtube angle. A 180mm dual crown is about the same axle to crown as a 170mm single crown though.
  • 30
englertracing (Nov 19, 2025 at 7:36)
 the headtube diameter can work with a DC, its just that the frame leading to it is too fat in your case.
  • 70
VorsprungSuspension (Nov 19, 2025 at 10:04)
 @cougar797: While you're correct in the normal bending sense, he's referring to crash damage from the uppers hitting the side of the frame behind the headtube. Good bumpers help but there's only so much they can do, frames aren't really built to take impacts there.
  • 15
Dougal-SC (Nov 19, 2025 at 11:07) (Below Threshold)
 @chakaping: 2/10 bait.
  • 20
Takaya94 FL (Nov 19, 2025 at 11:33)
 @Mtbdialed: Hows the Vaia been riding for you? Wondering how climbing would be with that beast up front. Such a sweet fork man. My Era V2 has been all I've really needed so far though.
  • 30
Takaya94 FL (Nov 19, 2025 at 11:38)
 @IsaacWislon82: Dude, you should post a pic of your rig with the Vaia. Good to know on the axle and flex part though. Think I'll score one and build a DH bike when I hit the lottery finally.
  • 20
Kilian1b15Gt (Nov 19, 2025 at 23:09)
 @cougar797: thats where you are wrong. Any crash where the handlebars turn a sufficient amount, like you lose front traction and fall, would put stress on an otherwise low stress part of the frame that only ever sees shear stresses spread across a large area and thus is rather thin. Ill use Santa Cruz as example since its easy to see and imagine there. The big flat section hebind the head tube where downtube and top tube join isnt built to handle impacts from the side whicht bump stops would do.
  • 12
cougar797 (Nov 20, 2025 at 5:40)
 @VorsprungSuspension: I guess i've never owned an enduro frame that had this limitation. I couldnt imagine owning one that a rock strike would kill that easy.
  • 20
IsaacWislon82 FL (Nov 20, 2025 at 11:11)
 @Takaya94: I put a bike check of it on the green site, here's the link : www.vitalmtb.com/community/isaacwilson/frameworks-dream-build

Takaya94 FL (Nov 20, 2025 at 12:19)

englertracing (Nov 21, 2025 at 9:43)

TommyNunchuck FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:18)
 Stop beating around the bush and give us dual crown Enduro forks instead of ridiculously huge crowns which creak anyway.
  • 1224
wolftwenty1 FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:21) (Below Threshold)
 People want this…? Seems everyone is going to less travel these days…
  • 453
thenotoriousmic (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:36)
 @wolftwenty1: yes we absolutely do want this. There’s no chance that someone couldn’t design a triple crowned lyrik / 36 that isn’t stiffer and lighter than a 38 or Zeb. Weak point is the crown steerer joint. Doesn’t make a difference how big the stantions are and how beefy the crown is you’re still going to get a load of flex and eventually creeking from the crown / steerer joint. All they’re doing is adding a bunch of weight for little to no improvement.
  • 90
iiman FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:54)
 @thenotoriousmic: post a review when you have a couple rides on
mrpbike.com/products/bartlett
  • 300
englertracing (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:54)
 @thenotoriousmic: Formula has one, dont forget the other benefits, direct mount stems, stiffer in sprinting with a 31.8 bar than a 35mm bar hanging from a 31.8 steerer, but softer in compression, multi holes on the clamps for dm stems allow testing of different stem lengths with one stem, full 1 1/8 steerer in a DC fork allows far more geo freedom with zs56 headsets, for reach and angle adjustments, front end height adjustment is nice too.... all with a stiffer package that doesn't creek.
  • 102
wolftwenty1 FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:57)
 @thenotoriousmic: Tripple crown...? lol.
  • 130
robotdave (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:57)
 @thenotoriousmic: Previous gen 35mm Boxxers are fantastic for this, I have one set at 180mm on my Transition Patrol as a park bike.
  • 150
shellbiketech (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:06)
 @wolftwenty1: Last winter I put a 40 on my Chilcotin and swore off single crown forks (Again). I got sick of not having the adjustability in travel and crown height. Let me tell you all... There is nothing like the safety and security of a dual crown. Steering is a non issue, I can adjust so much more in the front end, and if I wanted less travel I could just drop a smashpot in and go all the way down to 150mm. Right now its at 190mm and feelin' sweet. You all are missing out!

Caution: don't buy a 35mm boxxer. They are cheap and tempting but the charger 2.1 is turning into unobtanium. Seems they are unavailable anymore and no other good damper is available for them.

Nothing needs to change about DH forks for trailbike usage. They're light enough, and insanely good.
  • 280
NickBosshard (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:08)
 The Formula Belva exists, but nobody seems to actually want that. I work at the swiss distributor of Formula, and while the sales volume overall isn't huge, we see absolutely no interest. Their other forks which do have direct competitors sell in ok numbers, but not the Belva.
Maybe we just have to spraypaint them gold
  • 613
wolftwenty1 FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:19) (Below Threshold)

Bomberone FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 10:40)
 @NickBosshard: its a bit of a shame, because the Belva is brilliant. Bit i guess, the dual crown enduro thing is a extreme niche at the moment, even with competitive bikers.
  • 40
phops (Nov 18, 2025 at 11:04)
 The problem is frame manufacturers would have to update their frames to handle dual crown forks. Its not as simple as axle to crown. When fore/aft load is applied, the dual crown fork transfers all the stress into the headtube.
  • 81
ebikederangementsyndrome (Nov 18, 2025 at 11:07)
 @wolftwenty1: This doesn't really make any sense. Lift or shuttle access doesn't magically alter terrain
  • 16
wolftwenty1 FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 11:46) (Below Threshold)

NickBosshard (Nov 18, 2025 at 23:32)

shedfire (Nov 19, 2025 at 0:21)
 I really liked the Judy XL, but I'm very very old.
  • 10
dick-pound (Nov 19, 2025 at 1:12)
 @NickBosshard: the problem with the Belva is that:
1. It uses a PM160mm rotor mount, for a DC I expect no less than 200 nowadays
2. The air spring negative coil spring has a fixed rate, if the R version would have been an option probably it would have had better reviews
3. It costs no less than any other DH DC fork which also doesn't make it as marketable

The idea is good but the execution is not, otherwise we would have seen a lot more demand.
  • 15
TommyNunchuck FL (Nov 19, 2025 at 1:36) (Below Threshold)
 @NickBosshard: There will be a market for dual crown enduro forks when there is a good option. Belva is not it. Belva and Dorado both need an update.

Dougal-SC (Nov 20, 2025 at 0:52)
 @TommyNunchuck: they released 4 bolt drop crowns with 47mm offset a year or so back. All the listings and fitment info are on the shockcraft website. www.shockcraft.co.nz/manitou/fork-parts/crown-steerer-assembly-csa-csu?fork_model=1993

ljr20 FL (Nov 20, 2025 at 3:02)

iforte312 (Nov 20, 2025 at 7:45)
 @dick-pound: On thing I like about using a brake mount adapter on a fork is that it reduces the chance of stripping the threads in the magnesium lowers. We constantly working on brakes (especially centering the rotor, bleh), and I would rather strip a replaceable part.

TommyNunchuck FL (Nov 20, 2025 at 8:18)

wolftwenty1 FL (Nov 20, 2025 at 8:21)
 @englertracing: Oh...this fork is not in the consideration set...its a one-off dumb item nobody will buy...36/38 vs 40...its an easy choice .
  • 50
englertracing (Nov 20, 2025 at 11:13)
 @wolftwenty1: sorry im lost in different posts?

there are others, intend has the en, mojo had the morc which is way closer to one offs, mrp has the bartlett

ok a better comparison for readily avaliable forks would be a boxxer 35 at 180mm or 190mm and 2600 grams vs a zeb 170mm or 180mm at 2400g.

If you are winching and plummeting at appropriate trails that call for 180+ and you don't do tailwhips why would you choose the zeb? for turnbars?

same bike now, but as a bike park only rig, is the boxxer magically now right when it wasn't for the first case? do you really think it somehow disqualifies a bike for enduro use..... why?

on a dh bike? everyone universally agrees a dual crown is the way to go.

but how about a 55lb e bike, that many people use to access trails where DH bikes are ridden? and the extra heft of the E bike is now in play, and the precieved or mental "penalty" of having to pedal with a dual crown that that somehow makes pedaling worse because they are associated with dh bikes (complete bullshit if the weight is the same) is mitigated by the e bike. does it make sense to use a dual crown fork in this case? (id say 100% when i started seeing kenevos with boxxers and 40s i was like yeah... duh....), its absolutely as strong a use case as a DH bike.

btw I have a Dual crown 36 on my enduro, Morc clamps & tubes, with my damper and air spring systems because I appreciate the benefits and geometry adjustment freedom of the dual crown it weighs 50g more than a 38, its on a spindrift, i wouldn't do one on my ultra light trail bike though.

PS if single crowns were better wouldn't dh bikes use 42 or 45mm single crowns? would that make a dh bike now pedal able especially if the solution was heavier than a 40 dual crown?
  • 10
ljr20 FL (Nov 20, 2025 at 15:15)
 @TommyNunchuck: I think Dave Rulezman has stopped making the 47mm offset drop crown, could be worth sending him a message though on instagram
  • 50
FlatSkilz FL (Nov 20, 2025 at 23:28)
 Way back in the 2000s Manitou had the Sherman Slider triple clamp fork. It had 32mm stanchions, 20mm axle, titanium coil and also a air valve that influenced the bottom out resistance. It came in either 152mm or 170mm travel. My 2004 Giant AC1 came with the 152mm and performed better than my 2025 ZEB Ultimate at 170mm does now. The Sherman was extremely stiff and supple (obviously being coil). I wish they made forks as good as they used to.
  • 20
Pkge (Nov 21, 2025 at 15:31)

Dougal-SC (Nov 21, 2025 at 17:36)
 @Pkge: I ran Manitou Xvert DC forks at 145mm from 2000-2004. I ran an Xvert Carbon on the same turner when I wanted a bit more travel.

I still ride a Dorado on my knolly sometimes.

Loved it and never found a corner I couldn't ride. Steering angles aren't that high even on switchbacks. The tightest stall out corner I can ride I can do on the Dorado or single crown. It's uphill so you can't slide it.

SJP (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:50)

preston67 (Nov 18, 2025 at 10:35)

mikekazimer FL Editor (Nov 18, 2025 at 10:45)

SJP (Nov 18, 2025 at 11:41)

boozed (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:24)

  • 41
phops (Nov 18, 2025 at 11:07)
 @Simann: Except for the part where lubricant can get into your brakes....

USD forks shouldn't seep unless there is dirt in the stanchion seals, at which point you should be able to use a seal cleaner hook to get it out.
  • 31
heinous (Nov 18, 2025 at 13:19)
 @phops: any stanchion brings a small amount of lube past the seal with it.
  • 60
Kiowa008 (Nov 18, 2025 at 17:02)
 The shop I’m with has sold 3 podiums off the shelf that have been fine and 1 was spec’d on an e-bike that was not fine. When we called Fox they were already tracking the issue and it took about a week/week and a half so pretty good for warranty work but still annoying on a 2k product. A typical case of “don’t buy the 1st gen of anything” I suppose. That said, I’m sure I’d be happy with either fork but would probably lean towards a push because coil
  • 330
jostaudt (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:13)
 Makes the Intend forks look like a pretty good deal. Can PB start including those in the comparisons?
  • 60
Simann (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:56)
 That is my next fork. Push 9.1 was thr best fork ive ridden, but could be lighter.
  • 60
belial901 (Nov 18, 2025 at 12:09)
 I can assure you, the edge is the best for ive ever ridden. I also had a total of 1 shiver and 3 dorados in the past. Actually one of the dorados is still in use.
  • 60
Zalgorithm (Nov 19, 2025 at 12:04)
 I agree. We need an Intend, Fox, and Push comparison. Also if Cane creek decides to release their inverted fork and Bright Racing restarts their operation, that would awesome too.
  • 301
alekstkach (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:07)
 I hardly ever post anything but given that I have not seen any one comparing this to a normal coil fork I wanted to share.
A few months ago I saw the reviews for the Podium: more sensitivity, more mid stroke more weight. Doesn't that sound like just a normal coil fork? So I got a Vorsprung Smashpot for my zeb and now will always have trouble going back to air on anything that isn't a light bike. Essentially I am trying to figure out how I can upgrade all of my forks to coil (except the one bike that I am trying to keep light). Even if you include the 500$ CAD for the Smashpot to a ZEB you are still cheaper, not quite as heavy, and have a dead simple fork to easily work on at home. My buddy did the same thing by just getting a cheap Z1 coil and he feels the same way. So I would really really like to see a comparison to almost any normal fork with a coil compared to these inverted forks. I haven't tried the new batch of inverted but coming from moto I can see some of the apeal. But a Zeb with a smashpot is soooooo good that I highly doubt the current inverted forks would be at all better.
  • 41
SCCC120 (Nov 18, 2025 at 10:49)
 I’m right there with ya, got a Z1 coil at 170 on the stumpy evo (with cascade link), and ain’t going back. Was only $350 on sale. It is so smooth and tracks extremely well. Less fatigued on descents, so simple and amazing.
  • 1416
Dougal-SC (Nov 18, 2025 at 12:14)
 Spring makes more difference when you're going slow, damper makes more difference when you're going fast.

Put a coil spring in any fork with a sub-par damper and it'll feel nice in the carpark but still choke on every square hit and feel rough and unstable at speed. Sound familiar?
  • 50
heinous (Nov 18, 2025 at 13:22)
 One thing that is in the Push fork’s favour is how quick and easy it is to service and adjust travel.

Not a thing for lots of people, but if you swap between bikes, swap travel on a bike etc it’s a $0, 10 min job vs a complex, expensive job on a Smashpot, or a new air spring on other forks.
  • 20
alekstkach (Nov 18, 2025 at 19:45)
 @heinous: Don't really understand what you mean about the smashpot being difficult or expensive. It is really easy and cheap to change the travel as well as shim the HBO or swap springs. I'm not amazing with bike repair and I have done it many times without any issues just because I wanted to try different set ups. You do not even have to take the fork off or remove the lowers. Just tap out the spring side, collect the oil (or not) make the change and put it back in. Takes me about 30min and I am not fast with this sort of thing
  • 90
VorsprungSuspension (Nov 18, 2025 at 19:58)
 @alekstkach: I think Heinous is referring to frequent back-and-forth travel swaps. The Smashpot is certainly not expensive to do that with (all the required spacers come with it, and a piece of heat shrink needs replacing) and it’s not a massive job IMO, but it’s also not so fast/convenient that many people are just going to bump the fork up for a weekend at the bike park either.
  • 30
heinous (Nov 18, 2025 at 20:52)
 @VorsprungSuspension: yes, relatively speaking the push is super easy and fast. I really love the Smashpot and to be honest if the nine one had a pneumatic bottom out like the ACS I probably would have used a Smashpot for the HBO alone, it’s a superb design.
  • 4625
Kruetters (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:07)
 I've been running this fork since the spring and my experience doesn't fall in line with good portion of what's conveyed in this post...

Context:
I am 210lbs riding the North Shore 4-6 times weekly - I moved to this fork from a 38. I have not yet put Torque Caps on my front hub. Its on a 170mm WAO Arrival.

Fore/Aft stiffness:
I found with a 38 that in straight line braking the fork would flex inwards towards the bike whereas with the Push there is basically no Fore/Aft flex which really makes me question Mikes 160lbs commenting on flex of the fork under "compressions". Off of drops or repeated straight line hits I have not felt the fork to waver or feel any different than a 38 other than that it doesn't dive as much or flex under braking as badly as the 38.

Torsional Stiffness:
When it comes to torsional stiffness I have never found there to be a mushy feel or suddenly veering off line after an unexpected slip on something or encountering something off-camber or with a poor line choice or any discomfort on slow speed moves on rocky/rooty sections.I have yet to put Torque Caps on my hub as mentioned above.

If you take the front wheel between your legs and twist the bars you can get some torsional flex out of the fork which is obviously more than a traditional single crown but again this is not something that caused a noticeably different feel for me than a 38. The only time I've had that torsional or lateral flex feeling with a fork was a 180mm Fox 36 (and dont get me going about the awful Fore/Aft stiffness on that fork).

The fact that this review doesn't even aim to separate Torsional vs Fore/Aft stiffness is quite interesting to me.

Weight:
Of course the Push is going to weigh more than a Podium - 200-300grams - given that its coil. However a Smashpot upgrade for any 38/Zeb is going to add 250-450 grams and put you very near the same weight as this fork anyways.

Price:
What Mike doesn't mention that the fork has a lifetime warranty and given its modular format can/will be updated with various standards as they come out - as we've already seen them able to make the fork into a 27.5 version just by swapping the dropouts.

Also just get them when they go on sale and pay the same price as a Podium - as I did last spring.


Conclusion:
Maybe the odd "wandering" feel you felt with low speed maneuvers is as a result of you not being used to the damper feel? Does it make the front end of the bike feel muted? The level of small bump sensitivity certainly does take some getting used to.

I've always been skeptical about brake reviews from 160lb riders but now I have someone who's 50lbs lighter than I telling me that a fork is noticeably flexible when I haven't felt that at all... IDK Mike
  • 70
Kruetters (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:17)
 Oh and my only gripe with this fork is that the fender is 2 inches too short on the back end to stop things being thrown into the back of the bottom headset.
  • 3931
xciscool FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:23)
 “I spent $2k on a fork so of course it’s much better and I’m skeptical of anyone that’s says otherwise”
  • 180
YokoOno (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:26)
 100% My experience, and thoughts, exactly. I don't agree with Mike's review at all, in terms of the stiffness.

Yes, I've had mine with i9 with and wo torque caps. I do notice the difference and prefer it with TC
  • 235
Kruetters (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:27)
 @xciscool: I've owned/ridden plenty of other forks and my experience as a much heavier rider doesn't fall in line with what's said here so I took a moment to share that. I am more than willing to point out issues with anything I spend money on so that others don't waste theirs if there's an actual issue with it.

You ok bro?
  • 110
sfeeze FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:35)
 In the video review, Kaz very clearly (and repeatedly) separates torsional stiffness from fore / aft.
  • 102
Kruetters (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:37)
 @GoldenTinker: I can't listen to/watch the video right now - I figured the write up would cover all bases that the video did.
  • 130
gzaaaarrrggghhh (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:39)
 FWIW I entirely agree with you. For me the only negative is the price, and the fender which is a joke IMO. I’ve noticed that the 9.1 enables me to stay on a higher line on off camber roots, where other forks tend to pull you down the camber. We have a lot of roots here and this fork is unreal good on them. The build quality puts the rest of the industry to shame, no binding bushings here. The only negative in daily use is if you remove your front wheel to put your bikes on a rack or some such, this gets tedious very quickly - with a heavy e-bike putting the wheel back in has almost reduced me to tears. As the legs have no bridge and the machining tolerances are so high you have to have the legs EXACTLY aligned for the axle to go through, which is easier said than done!
  • 21
ebikepartyshirt (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:44)
 The video goes in depth about the difference in fore-aft flex vs torsional flex.
  • 164
mikekazimer FL Editor (Nov 18, 2025 at 10:08)
 @Kruetters, it's surprising that you don't notice the additional flex, but if it's working for you that's great. Like I mentioned in the review, the grip is excellent, and plowing through the chunk in a straight line is its strong suit - there are plenty of trails on the Shore where I can see this being an excellent option.

The weight and the lack of stiffness in tighter, steeper sections of trail are what hold it back for me when talking solely about performance (the price is also going to be a tough one for a lot of riders).
  • 21
Kruetters (Nov 18, 2025 at 11:36)
 @mikekazimer: Ill have to get out to some more 7th, Pipeline, Boundary laps but while I did notice an awkward feeling on some of those slower corners it felt to me more like i couldnt feel the input of the trail rather than any actual drifting of the front end.

Im actually of a mind to strap a gopro to the headtube and go for a ride.

Yeah I wouldn't have bought it if it wasn't on sale.
  • 60
phops (Nov 18, 2025 at 11:40)
 I had a dorado (non carbon version) on my DH bike. The torsional stiffness is definitely noticeable. Its actually really good in rocky sections, as the fork tends to track better, but when doing high speed berms, the front feels very understeery.

Personally, instead of running this, I would just set up a semi-dh bike with a dual crown air fork for more trail use. Canfield One.2, Norco Shore, e.t.c. The large volume air chamber of a dual crown means that the fork is very linear, you get more travel, more adjustability in front ride height, and good stiffness all around.
  • 46
Mtbdialed (Nov 18, 2025 at 12:43)
 man the brake thing hit home! reading so many gushing reviews of Mavens and then actually spending some time with some.....jesus christ are they pathetic. same SRAM sponge feeling in the lever, same lack of power, won't hold a bleed. just ass.....both cheeks. binned them and tossed on some V4 Hopes. Night and day doesn't even come close to adequately describing the difference. Seriously SRAM needs to just shutter their brake dept. they acquired Avid decades ago and didn't change a single thing. lol
  • 101
stevemokan (Nov 18, 2025 at 13:29)
 @Mtbdialed: sounds like they weren't bled properly.
  • 60
heinous (Nov 18, 2025 at 15:36)
 The Arrival is the perfect bike for the Nine One - the ability to change travel quickly is so well suited to a fork that's so easy to service and swap travel.

I got a Nine:one at a modest discount specifically due to the travel / service ease, and Push' fantastic support. With modern bikes the chance of needing warranty is high, that's just how it is, but with SRAM products in particular, and Fox to a certain extent, the odds of actually getting half decent support is really limited. It's so frustrating having a bike that can't be ridden because a manufacturer won't support their products. I avoided Intend due to feedback from friends not liking the dampers, and the relatively limited support here.

My Nine:one replaced a 170mm 38 when I was running the bike at 170mm and a 160mm 36 when running bike at 130 or 140mm. I've previously run Smashpots and absolutely love them, with the major problem being it's not viable to swap travel with them quickly. The Push is less expensive when it's replacing two forks.

As mentioned in the article, the hub axle makes a massive difference. I wouldn't be entirely surprised if push offered a set of 20mm lugs and axle as an option in future, but for me (82kg) the torsional flex is not something I've noticed negatively. I do think the way the fork smooths things it takes some time to decipher they feel and front end traction compared to other forks.

With the sub chamber, I've found I can run one spring weight lighter (I was borderline and opted heavier).
  • 11
Bradleybourbon (Nov 18, 2025 at 16:55)
 @YokoOno: likewise
  • 74
Simann (Nov 18, 2025 at 18:17)
 Same! A year on my Push 9.1 and i've never experienced that "wandering" feeling. I think him switching front wheels (hubs) is the issue. I guarantee having such a stiff fork like the 9.1 that an alloy rim on butted spokes and 24 or 28H would really exaggerate the lack of torsional rigidity of the alloy wheel. And if you swap to a carbon front wheel on the I9 hub and 28 or 32H, the ride would be far more torsionally stiff.
I vividly remember taking the 9.1 and trying to flex fork legs apart and it was nearly impossible. But take a 38 and you can twist those legs pretty easily.
I've yet to ride a Podium, and if the Podium rides like a 38, I really could care less. You just can't get the ride quality that the Push coil fork, or any coil fork of that matter, has. For us guys with some older wrists and arthritis, the coil fork is a savior.
  • 59
Mtbdialed (Nov 18, 2025 at 18:46) (Below Threshold)
 @stevemokan: Factory sealed mate!

also, they did lose bleed every 3-5 days, in which I would re-do, then they would go pearshaped again in a few days.

they just f*cking suck and everyone needs to admit it, regardless of how well you would like them to work.

P.s. hit up my buy sell if you want all the take offs my customers have swapped out!!!
  • 40
heinous (Nov 18, 2025 at 19:17)
 @mikekazimer: what are your thoughts on starting to objectively measure this stuff? It’s not expensive or complex to build a small jig to measure fire / aft and torsional flex, and then instead of arguing about how much flex there is in the comments we can…

…argue about something else?
  • 20
ebikepartyshirt (Nov 18, 2025 at 20:05)
 @heinous: problem is, those flex data points could be misleading. Push doesn’t deny that the fork flexes torsionally, but they do say it doesn’t matter and it adds grip. Sometimes focussing on a specific data point can go poorly. Look at the race to have light equipment.
  • 50
heinous (Nov 18, 2025 at 20:56)
 @BrianColes: sure, but if it’s a consistent data point that’s well thought out then you can talk about it effectively in a way that ‘my feels vs your feels’ makes impossible. It would also show data across a range of forks in a way that could be quite insightful.

Fore aft is straight forward, torsional may take a little more thought given the system axle is integral to it.
  • 66
lkubica (Nov 18, 2025 at 23:53)

No offence but the weight is just one factor, the other is speed and riding ability. Now, you can be a great rider or a sucker which I do not know and so I choose to believe Mike, no offence.

frorider2 (Nov 19, 2025 at 6:21)
 @heinous: I agree with you, the German review sites often measure engineering variables like torsional stiffness and then see whether that correlates to the qualitative reviewer impressions. This type of approach is just basic science. Fox said they did this when they tried to determine the optimum stiffness of the Podium. Pinkbike has for years just avoided making that effort. Laziness?

phastlikedatnascar FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 11:54)

  • 2128
Dougal-SC (Nov 18, 2025 at 12:15) (Below Threshold)
 @phastlikedatnascar: The Dorado is that far ahead of every other DH fork that it's not even funny. All the advertisers know this.

Almost waiting for the "it felt too good so we added 30% more pressure then it felt funny and it bounced off all the rocks" review like they did for the Mezzer.
  • 453
mikekazimer FL Editor (Nov 18, 2025 at 12:55)
 @Dougal-SC, how are you still harping on that Mezzer review? It was seven years ago. That test fork had issues, including significant bushing play. Since then, I've reviewed the Mezzer Expert and was impressed: www.pinkbike.com/news/review-manitou-mezzer-expert-fork.html.
  • 104
phops (Nov 18, 2025 at 13:03)
 @Dougal-SC: Dorardo is a tank. 3000 grams with carbon chassis. Not exactly a nice thing to have a bike weighing as much as an ebike without a motor.

Also its not without issues. For one, I had to tighten the pinch bolts harder than recommended torque specs, because simply rough riding would cause twist in the chassis enough to make the bars point not straight. Secondly, the seals on the stanchions are really tight (so that oil doesn't seep). So tight that at Angel Fire in NM (10k ft altitude), me and a few others ended up blowing the seal straight out due to air pressure buildup, and it doesn't have a way to bleed the lowers.
  • 101
MorganBH FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 13:28)
 @phops: my dorado has little bolts on top to bleed the uppers. It's less than 100 grams heavier than the push. It's also plenty stiff for me at 210 lbs riding pretty chonky stuff in the pnw
  • 155
ChupacabraOffroad (Nov 18, 2025 at 13:44)
 @mikekazimer: is this true @Dougal-SC ? 7 years? Get a life bro
  • 153
heinous (Nov 18, 2025 at 14:14)
 @ChupacabraOffroad: guys, this isn’t about the review, that’s just the vehicle for Dougal to get attention.
  • 2028
Dougal-SC (Nov 18, 2025 at 14:50) (Below Threshold)
 @mikekazimer: 6 years ago for the Pro review. 5.5 years for the Expert review. Nothing since.

59psi on the Expert, 54/84psi in the Pro. Same rider, same chassis. That's a massive overspringing on the Pro which torpedo's the review.

The bushing play (oval bushing seat in the castings) was the only issue and one to affect all major brands with new lower legs from 2018-2020. Because they all used the same lower leg manufacturer. We heard nothing about the other brand bushings on any channel and curiously never do.

@phops The Dorado is the same weight as this fork. The top-caps have pressure vent screws to fix the pressure build-up. There is a 4 bolt upper crown released about a year ago to stop them twisting. The seals are the same 37mm SKF wiper as Mezzer.
  • 1021
Dougal-SC (Nov 18, 2025 at 15:02) (Below Threshold)
 4.5 years ago for the Expert. Edit button disappeared on me.

phops (Nov 24, 2025 at 12:57)
 @Mtbdialed: Still heavy AF.

I get why people don't find it a big deal, because the average crowd wants to basically ride DH trails at average pace while letting the suspension eat everything. But once you start to appreciate responsiveness and the ability to just gap over stuff without having to yank on 3kgs of weight, you realize that you don't necessarily need that much suspension.

Ive been on Formula Nero C for a year which with coil weighs as much as a Fox 40, and its been fantastic. Also run Ohlins 36 instead of 38 on the enduro bike for same reason.

Mtbdialed (Nov 24, 2025 at 16:30)

phops (Nov 24, 2025 at 20:16)

phops (Nov 24, 2025 at 20:54)

MorganBH FL (Nov 24, 2025 at 21:01)
 @phops: it's weird, I can pull gaps with my big, heavy DC fork just fine.
  • 160
Daray FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 14:36)
 I've been riding on a nine.one for over a year and have tested it on multiple bikes at both 160 and 170 travel configurations.I currently have it on a Santa Cruz Nomad. I love this Fork. It's like a cheat code for traction both climbing and descending. I personally have liked the slight flex in the fork as I feel it gives me more traction again.
Regarding torsional flex: I have noted a couple of instances over the year, where I felt like the torsional flex was more than I wanted it to be. It was usually on a steep rock section with a sharp corner at the bottom. Hi-line in Sedona comes to mind. But honestly I'll take that drawback all day long for the increased performance everywhere else.

The weight isn't noticeable at all to me. Like going from a 36 to a 38, you feel it the first time you pull for a manual and then never again.

I'll also agree that it's very expensive, and it doesn't make me a faster rider, but I do like how it rides and it is super easy to service.

I plan to keep this Fork for many years to come.
  • 150
islandforlife FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:22)
 Hey Fox, that’s how you design a fender for an inverted fork. The current fender for the podium makes your bike look like that ‘80’s moto knockoff Zellers bike.

Ok maybe that’s a bonus?

stormracing FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:38)

ebikepartyshirt (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:46)

Ty927 (Nov 18, 2025 at 10:19)
 @stormracing: The patent system is so f*cking dumb

smegman (Nov 18, 2025 at 10:55)
 @Ty927: Yes it is when it's about a simple protective piece of plastic that has nothing revolutionary.
  • 42
Mtbdialed (Nov 18, 2025 at 12:37)
 @Ty927: you are right. its the dumbest system....aside from any other system to protect IP ever devised. lol

sure, you might hate it, and it has its flaws, but without a way to recoup your R&D investment before someone else is allowed to just steal your shit, then zero innovation happens.
  • 74
Dougal-SC (Nov 18, 2025 at 14:59)
 @stormracing: How could this be patented when Manitou did the same thing 4 years ago for the Dorado?

sudochuckwalla (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:09)
 Its a very cool looking fork, but I couldn't see myself owning one. The performance, price, and weight just don't make sense when you can get a 38/zeb for so much less. Its cool it exists though.

Simann (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:55)
 Having ridden a 9.1 for over a year, its worth the money and you dont notice the weight like you think you would.
  • 122
natec4 FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 13:25)
 I picked up a V1 Nine One on Pink Bike marketplace this summer for my Transition Relay and have been extremely impressed. Looking forward to the V2 upgrades that I'll have installed over the winter, but for me, a 185lb rider based in Colorado, it has handled everything from Dirt Merchant at Whistler to gnarly tech lines in the high alpine to freeride skree lines without any issue. I will say that on big huck to flats that it does blow through the travel pretty quickly, but that should all be addressed with the V2 upgrades based on what I've read. I also noticed a signifcant improvement in tortional stiffness by using Torque Caps and moving from a Stans M-pulse front hub to a Hope Pro5. Yes it's expensive, but it's worth it to me. 100% Amercian made by a small team of talented engineers and machinists based in CO. I'd buy another one just purely based on the ease of 50hr service intervals alone. I can't believe @mikekazimer didn't mention the quick bleed / service advantage in any capacity
  • 142
devinkalt (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:02)
 What matters most is that it’s controversial. Not just another flat pedal but a rival for the Fox Podium
  • 111
KUBBY FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:09)
 Have you guys ever done a review / ridden the Vorsprung Smashpot coil conversion kit? I’d be curious on your thoughts compared to this.
  • 81
rojo-1 (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:31)
 Yep, Zeb vorsprung eats small bumps at a fraction of the price. How can you not compare to this...
  • 21
alekstkach (Nov 18, 2025 at 19:50)
 Totally agree. So much of the USD goals are essentially what a coil does anyway
  • 11
chakaping FL (Nov 19, 2025 at 1:25)
 This is what I thought when the Podium came out as well. If we're adding weight and seeking increased sensitivity anyway, surely that's exactly what a coil does - but much more simply?
  • 12
wutamclan (Nov 19, 2025 at 13:40)
 Not a scientific test, but I demoed a dual crown bike at the bike park. Granted rental bikes aren't set up perfectly, but I got a pretty good idea how the suspension worked. A few week later, I return with my Smashpot bike with Fox DHX coil shock. To my relief, all the money I spent upgraded suspension paid off! My 160/150 bike felt smoother on brake bumps than the DH 180 travel rental bike.
  • 90
belial901 (Nov 18, 2025 at 12:10)
 The obvious comparison should've been an intend, not that fox garbage. Would love to read that eventually. I for my self am very happy with my Edge and sad that it outperforms my dorado RRT.
  • 81
Simann (Nov 19, 2025 at 8:04)
 Torsional flex is not a bad thing. And its intentionally engineered into the Push fork for very specific reasons. There will always be compromises, and some may do fine with them and others may complain. But for the masses, I think Push knows their general demographic and designed the coil fork for them.

Please FFW to 10min in, Push CEO Darren Murphy directly addressed it

www.vitalmtb.com/features/upside-down-push-nine-one-fork-finally-here
  • 81
jdunne (Nov 19, 2025 at 8:21)
 Mike Fair review. I am glad to see this fork reviewed. I have both the Push and Fox 38 X2. I greatly prefer Push. I can not dial in the Fox 38 with anything resembling the comfort and better feel of the Push I am on the heavy side. 200LBS. and my bike a Pivot Shuttle LT is not light either. I also had this fork on my Pivot Firebird. I am in So Cal, with very steep, rocky, loose over hardback trails. For me coil is just a better feel than Air. That really seems to be the conclusion of this review. If you like the stiffer, higher friction feel of air so be it. But if you want the best confidence inspiring feel on chunky, technical downhill. Coil rules. weight is really the big consideration. if you are on a emtb, then IMO weight is kind of meaningless. And this fork is great for the heavier setups. Frankly I like the feel of a heavier bike for downhill. It's a much more confidence inspiring feel. I can hit lines harder and have more control. Lighter bikes seem to get bounced all over the place. Less control. I remember Mike hated the Trust fork as well. I did not agree with Mike there either. I really liked the Trust fork. I would still be riding that fork, if the bike did not get stolen. So my riding style does not seem to match Mike's.
  • 60
stickman5000 (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:08)
 I'm no expert and probably don't know what I'm talking about but in the motocross world, the inverted fork was prefered because you could make it so much stiffer without crazy diameters, Showa tried a monster 50mm stanchion fork on Suzuki in 97 I believe and McGrath prefered and changed to an iverted fork. Seems other than to be cool and different, in the mtb world, the inverted fork is just the opposite of what it was designed to be.
  • 30
Bro-LanDog (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:33)
 An mx bike has ~305mm front travel, that much leverage on the fork may benefit from beefier upper tubes than a traditional fork. Maybe traditional mtb (single crown) forks being only 120-170mm there can be more design emphasis on torsional rigidity with an arch added vs the need to have beefier upper stanchions when a 200lb moto overshoots a 40ft jump. Just spill ballin', I also don't know what I'm talking about
  • 70
DylanJM77 (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:44)
 Non inverted motocross forks didn't have a lower arch connecting the lowers like mtb forks do so they were likely more flexy comparatively speaking. There's a lot more material on an inverted moto fork and they are dual crown compared to these single crown mtb forks. So the twisting flex likely isn't so apparent.
  • 10
englertracing (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:55)
 @DylanJM77: aftermarket fork arch braces were a thing...
  • 30
mountaindoctor (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:59)
 Don’t remember the exact numbers, but the vehicle-to-fork weight ratio is way lower on a MTB than a MX bike. To make a Podium with the same torsional stiffness of a 38, I am afraid it should weight lot more than 3kilo, matching the frame weight. Any engineer will be welcome to better explain the concept or prove me wrong :-)
  • 40
cotic-bikes (Nov 19, 2025 at 8:43)
 @Bro-LanDog: You're definitely on the right line there. The weight and loads on a moto mean the stiffness advantage of an upside down fork - namely the bigger upper tubes giving much better front-rear stiffness - is worth more than on a bicycle. Plus weight is less of an issue with an engine on board. The other thing is that motos aren't limited to stupid tiny front axle diameters. Put a 30mm axle in one of these and it would be markedly better in torsion, but you'd have to make a hub and your own rotors. Maverick and Foes both tried that and failed.
  • 14
Dougal-SC (Nov 19, 2025 at 12:06)
 It needs stated that moto forks are all dual crown. So the debate there is dual crown conventional vs dual crown inverted. The few who tried a single crown moto fork gave up immediately.
  • 90
showmethemountains (Nov 18, 2025 at 12:47)
 With the modular design of this fork, Push could release 20mm dropouts as a future upgrade and/or purchase option, right?
  • 82
8Matt3 (Nov 18, 2025 at 13:37)
 For my experience with the fork, I was always hesitant money wise to spend that amount and it can be looked at as a downside. But, I’m a firm believer in you get what you paid for. If, you can AFFORD it. Key word right there. Seeing issues with the podium I wasn’t going to go that route…

I’m 5’7” 170ish pounds and ride a Santa Cruz Bullit. I’m heavy footed, gravity driven and I tend to be hard on bikes/parts. I know what works for me.

I’ve had the stock suspension professionally tuned by fluid focus and absolutely loved how the bike felt and I never thought that I could get it any better. I had some issues along the way with parts wearing out quickly because of age so, I was turned on to PUSH. I initially only wanted to get a rear shock but a friend said I needed to give the fork a shot as well. So, I was in a position to where I could afford it and I love giving new innovative things a try so I pulled the trigger.

I’ll cut this story shorter but, my first ride I wanted to find a trail I knew and had a variety of everything. I was blown away on the performance of both the fork and rear coil right out of the box. It literally transformed my bike for the better. I went from pre-runner to Baja truck. This is what I personally like out of my Bullit. I do need to dispel that the fork has any kind of flex, I haven’t had any issues with flex. I run no torque caps on my hubs and haven’t had any issues. I do want to give it a try and see if it makes any difference to ride quality. But, zero issues. It’s hard to describe but if you ever have the chance to ride this fork you’ll understand the feeling.

I’m not one to have issues with a review but I do believe in what push suspension is doing as a company. No issues, high quality, ease of service and customer service. Great job PUSH!
  • 100
schihim (Nov 18, 2025 at 11:59)
 i want intend review
  • 80
sspiff FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 16:16)
 push USD fork on a yeti e-bike. If that's not a Colorado Cadillac, I don't know what is.
  • 61
IsaacWislon82 FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 18:05)
 One big impression I've developed on inverted forks since building up a DH bike with the new EXT Vaia:

The inherent lower torsional flex is pretty much only an issue for higher level riders, especially those with a more physical style. If you can't push a bike hard enough to feel it flex it doesn't matter. The increased suppleness however, benefits all skill levels.

And to be clear I do absolutely love the EXT fork, but it has its tradeoffs, notably in very high g-force berms, stoppie-steep tech, and big sideways jump landings. But it is miles better everywhere else
  • 61
jdunne (Nov 19, 2025 at 7:59)
 It needs to be emphasized is that while the initial cost of this fork is not cheap. This fork will last a long time over many bikes. Like the shock, You can get it overhauled for your new bike.

I have moved my Push shock to 3 different bikes now. each time I send it back to push for a reasonably priced re-valve/service.

This makes the cost a LOT more competitive

atgni67 (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:24)

irapko (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:29)
 This will be great for the Crestline riding Silicon Valley dads that don't have enough skill to make the fork flex.
  • 51
sadokist FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 10:41)
 Honestly, after riding EXT for for last 4 years, I just want something that is consistent and doesn't blow up every two months (and then cost $900+ to have the fork and shock serviced each time). Boutique is fun, and when my stuff is working well its great, but not so great that it is worth the extra investment, and it is worse when it is blown up (which is more often than not).
  • 71
extracingshox (Nov 20, 2025 at 0:28)
 Hello,
we’re really sorry to hear about your experience. We’d be happy to learn more about the reliability issues you encountered so we can better understand what happened. If you’d like, you can reach us directly at mtb@extremeshox.com
Of course, if our products were failing every two months on a regular basis, it would be a much more widespread issue, so we’d really like to look into your specific case.
Regarding the $900 cost you mentioned, it seems a bit higher than the service prices listed by our Canadian distributor, so happy to know also about that.
Andrea
  • 30
sadokist FL (Nov 20, 2025 at 12:58)
 @extracingshox: Thanks for reaching out. I will email you my service history, but it's not pretty.
  • 51
KalleStrom FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 23:50)
 Unfortunately for Push they are still a head of the real USD trend. Right now we only want super stiff forks ,compliant bars and flexy rear ends. Give it a year or to and no one will be able to ride without that lateral flex of the fork thats "just keeps the front end hugging the ground...."
  • 84
wickyfelps (Nov 19, 2025 at 14:27)
 Wow! I think this review resulted in more reader engagement than any article I’ve seen in a while. Push suspension is not perfect but it’s really really good. Designed, manufactured, assembled and maintained in the good ol US. The quality is just so much higher than the other big brands. Have a question, call and they answer immediately. Darren will even answer the phone sometimes. At what other suspension company do you get to talk to the owner? At this point in my life, I’m tired of cheap, lightweight, plasticy crap that is sold at a premium. Push is the opposite of this. My approach is to save up a little money, buy the highest quality item once, maintain it and you’ve got it for life. I’m too poor to buy low quality stuff.
  • 50
Bike-JAM-AMA (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:37)
 20 mm vs 15 mm axle. Its funny on the Fox podium 20 mm was a big negative for some. And now 15 mm is big negative because it isn't stiff enough.
  • 160
Mtmw (Nov 18, 2025 at 10:33)
 welcome to pinkbike
  • 41
mprett1 (Nov 18, 2025 at 10:42)
 It's good at least to see companies like Push putting the innovations out there. Understood it is pricey and may not be for everyone now, but my hopes are in a few years, there are hyper-stiff, inverted forks that are reasonably priced.

This seems like part of the process/evolution to get there.
  • 30
corerider (Nov 18, 2025 at 16:26)
 I like the top crown support, and mostly like the 15mm/110 axle which makes it far more compatible with other forks should you not like it, unlike Fox which uses the DH standard of 20mm/110. Personally I don't understand why they got rid of 20mm when it worked just fine and was a standard all could agree upon. But this is biking we are talking about.
  • 31
jdejace (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:38)
 A very cool effort by a small company and I love the in-house manufacturing. Push make nice stuff and I have really liked my SV8. I suspect most (ie average) riders would enjoy this fork. For faster or heavier riders it's hard to beat physics.

I wonder if the Push fork might shine brightest on a mid travel steel frame with a bit of flex eg Starling, for riders on natural tech trails at moderate grades. Some things that might put it over the top are better reliability than the Podium (TBD) and a 20mm axle.

Personally as a ~200lb slightly faster than average rider who gets a dozen or so bike park days per season before switching to skis, I just wish more companies would embrace dual crowns for longer travel enduro bikes.
  • 70
stevemokan (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:51)

sanchofula (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:41)

Pick all three

joelsman (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:38)
 Z1 coil with a damper upgrade is lighter and less expensive. I love my z1coil, even though I still have the grip damper. Jeff Kendleweed has a review of a z1coil with better damper

DMFT FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 11:12)
 Nirvana t-shirts never went outta style Kaz. You take that back!!
  • 31
pokbud (Nov 18, 2025 at 15:10)
 Cheap 38 mm chassis Smashpot Lift V2 All the performance of the hydraulic bottom out and a stiff chassis with a damper tuned to your weight for a half of the price. I think push needs to go back to custom tuning off the shelf. Shocks.....
  • 60
bitterbiker (Nov 18, 2025 at 19:31)
 Interesting how Trials motorcycles don't use inverted forks
  • 10
jackfunk (Nov 21, 2025 at 7:36)
 I assume it’s due to conventional forks being more compact, and having the stantions further from obstacles
  • 20
Orangewp (Nov 22, 2025 at 4:50)
 We were fortunate enough to buy an Amflow PL carbon with Push fork and shock already fitted,the guy had already changed the wheels (hydra classic hubs and DT Swiss ex rims in mx size).We paid less for the bike than you could purchase a new standard Amflow and it had only been ridden less than 250 klms.As I said we were very lucky to have bought it as it was as I certainly couldn’t afford either the fork or shock as upgrade items.Previous to riding this bike my son had only ever ridden Orange bikes,Patriot in his early years and two stage 6 up to the present time.Bought the very first Zeb when they were released and used the same fork on both stage 6 bikes after numerous lower leg services and yearly services by TF tuned.To someone who had only ever ridden that fork it was the best experience for him but after riding the Push suspended Amflow it was so different.Coil sensitivity both ends and as Mike commented very plush.He’s a decent rider coming from a motocross background for about 15 years and he’s never complained about the flex problem Mike raises.There was however one problem with the fork,it bottomed out every now and again and not always on landing off big jumps,some of you may have guessed already but after numerous adjustments with high speed comp the problem couldn’t be ironed out so to say.The “fix”…..V2 upgrade,new sub chamber system with hydraulic bottom out.Supplied and fitted by TF but not yet tested,just our experience with the fork and before all you Orange haters start chiming in about bad those bikes are,if it’s all you’ve ridden they are the best as far as you’re concerned,they have their problems as do many other brands but the customer service was and still is brilliant.The “we” are my son,the rider and I’m his old man that doesn’t ride anymore after a spinal injury but just maintains and looks after his bikes,not rich or has a trade in dentistry.
  • 84
letsridemtb (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:32)
 "It's coil sprung, upside down, heavy, and expensive." They lost me right there.
  • 62
Batipapo (Nov 18, 2025 at 10:00)
 Found a Fox38 on sale for $379 and it’s an amazing fork/tuning. How can people sell these forks for soo much more.

Cord1 (Nov 18, 2025 at 10:00)
 Why are the boxxer and 40 not part of this review? Seems like they’d win on all counts. (Apart from X-ups, and I haven’t done one of those in many years.)
  • 41
hitarpotar (Nov 18, 2025 at 12:43)
 Someone please explain to me why I would choose a Podium or this Push instead of a Dorado which has more travel, is less flexi, costs less, and looks better (in my view)!
  • 30
Kilian1b15Gt (Nov 19, 2025 at 2:28)
 Dual crown approval. Dual crown forks can more easily damage your frame in a crash. the rubber bump stops push against rather thin parts of the frame in a direction where such loads were never intended to be. That is especially true for carbon frames. For example Santa Cruc frames are pretty flat behind the head tube, a good enogh crash may cause cracks in that area
  • 51
gbcarmona (Nov 18, 2025 at 13:47)
 I remember a simple time when a Fox 36 was all the fork anyone would ever need!

chakaping FL (Nov 19, 2025 at 7:46)

Sethimus (Nov 18, 2025 at 13:48)
 Now do the Intend Flash 38!

colebmx (Nov 19, 2025 at 0:02)

Lousicle (Nov 19, 2025 at 6:22)
 Saw the price…. Instantly not interested
  • 40
hubertje-ryu (Nov 19, 2025 at 9:54)

englertracing (Nov 19, 2025 at 17:20)

hubertje-ryu (Nov 23, 2025 at 4:14)
