kawin20 (Nov 19, 2025 at 12:41)
 The high weight of the Nine One is noticeable, especially on a bike without a motor. .............We are generational LOW.... Pedal a Dam Bike this now the softest generation ever
  • 32
llaskin (Nov 21, 2025 at 11:50)
 The timing of this review with this QuarterHP
episode was great to put the 2 somewhat different conclusions in relief: www.youtube.com/watch?v=lHuEfR3pyIg

There's was also Cornelius from Intend who posted this: "This isn’t meant as criticism of that article or its author. Instead, it’s a different perspective — and an invitation to reflect on how we all form opinions about products. How much of what we believe comes from our own neutral evaluation, and how much is shaped by expectations built from past articles, stories, or assumptions?
Yesterday was a bad day for upside down forks. A major online magazine stated that the Push 9.1 fork was not stiff enough. That perception is true, no doubt about it. However here are some thoughts:
For 10 years I was alone thinking USD forks are nice. My company "Intend" is still existing, now going into the 9th year. USD forks are the product we are most famous for. I had to fight hard to convince people that USD is working. Before Intend there were USD forks from Manitou, Marzocchi, Rockshox, Magura and others. And after there were forks from Push, Fox, and forks from other brands are also in development. How can they build such forks, if they are too weak?
This year our forks won the German national title in the women & men elite fields. But yes, Germans probably don't ride so hard. Rapha scored a 2nd, 4th and 7th place at EDR this year. Lars & Moritz from Dust Factory Racing both placed several Top 30 results in EDR races. Max flat-out won an Open-EDR race. Moritz finished 2nd on an EDR stage. Our rider Vojta Blaha from Czech Republic scored a 24th overall at EDR and also won the national Enduro title. But yes, they probably all don't ride hard enough to feel the flex.
(This is of course a joke, they ride as hard as you can ride on an Enduro bike 🫶)
How is it possible we sold over 3000 forks over the past decade and we are still here?
Why don't we get a shitstorm from every customer buying one?
Maybe the article is wrong or I am, maybe it's also just personal preference acoording to ones previous experiences.
And yes, if it is a perception that USD forks are too flexy, we will change it,
Maybe not now, but in time.
My honest opinion:
"Compliance is not a problem.
It's a benefit."
- ᴄᴏʀɴᴇʟɪᴜꜱ ᴋᴀᴘꜰɪɴɢᴇʀ, ꜰᴏᴜɴᴅᴇʀ ᴏꜰ ɪɴᴛᴇɴᴅ"
  • 44
mikekazimer FL Editor (Nov 21, 2025 at 17:36)
 For the record, I'm not opposed to USD forks at all, and at no point have I said that I want the stiffest fork possible - a 36mm stanchioned, right side up fork feels fine to me in the vast majority of situations. However, with the Nine One, I found that the ride characteristics weren't as good as those of the 38, and I also preferred the Podium over the Nine One. There's an odd amount of finger-pointing and hand-wringing over this review, but like I said at the end of the video, I encourage people to try one out if they get a chance in order to see / feel for themselves.
  • 10
llaskin (Nov 25, 2025 at 23:59)
 @mikekazimer: True, you didn’t explicitly say you’re opposed to USD forks, nor did you really imply it. But you also weren’t enthusiastic about them. You don’t need to be enthusiastic, of course, but given the overwhelming enthusiasm among professional suspension engineers, many of whom see USD forks as the holy grail for the future of the sport, I don’t think it is surprising that your review has prompted so much finger-pointing and hand-wringing.
  • 10
uphillbob FL (Mar 12, 2026 at 13:52)
 I've come to the conclusion that I must appreciate different things in suspension than anybody here at pinkbike. I'm heavier than the reviewers, probably don't ride quite as hard but in the same ballpark based on videos, and all of the suspension products they like, I find far too jarring, ping-y, and generally uncomfortable and confidence un-inspiring when I push them hard. The suspension that I've loved and lets me push my bikes harder and feel much more in control are always called out as being "too flexy," "over damped," or something else along those lines. If you're somebody that likes comfort, big hit smoothness, and a trail-erasing feeling, probably take Mike and Dario's opinions with a grain of salt. Not saying their opinions are any less valid than mine, but I think they value a much different ride characteristic than myself and likely a lot of other riders do.
  • 75
wyorider (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:45)
 Heavy, expensive, and squishy in the right and wrong ways. Perfect for the well heeled out of shape broped rider to cruise their local blue trails at full throttle......
  • 41
novajustin (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:31)
 heavy and expensive in the cons column. installs the fork on one of the most expensive e bikes on the market. that tracks.
  • 40
fred-frod (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:52)
 Are "right side up" forks the same as "left side down"?
  • 10
valleybreezer (Nov 18, 2025 at 18:06)
 Depends if you're talking about the spring or the damper.
  • 65
Endo79 (Nov 18, 2025 at 10:04)
 Buy a second hand zeb, put a smashpot v2 in it. Cost half the price, be stiffer and just as supple. This inverted trend is trying to solve an issue thats already been solved in a better way.
  • 95
Mtbdialed (Nov 18, 2025 at 12:49)
 fore/aft stiffness is more important than torsional stiffness. THAT is what a USD solves that no amount of smashpot additions will address in a conventional fork.
  • 35
Endo79 (Nov 18, 2025 at 13:18)
 @Mtbdialed: but it then flexes in other ways you don't want it to. A zeb or fox 38 is not flexing in anyway that affects riders. It's all marketing and not solving an actual issue. The other issue fox says inverted is to make break out force less. Bigger bushes etc. hence get a current Enduro stiff fork and smashpot. Issue solved
  • 85
Dougal-SC (Nov 18, 2025 at 14:30)
 If you're just looking for coil spring then buy a Z1 coil for even less. It's the damper that makes the ride and all those have issues.
  • 22
Endo79 (Nov 19, 2025 at 5:00)
 @Dougal-SC: strongly disagree. The v2 smashpot is a much better thing having tried both. Having the two different springs plus the hydraulic bottom out. As for inverse fork, they won't ever work in mtb. Top heavy, big negatives and the issues they claim to solve can be solved with existing tech for far less money and weight
  • 11
Comatosegi (Nov 19, 2025 at 6:05)
 @Endo79: Definitely a place for USD forks. Podium has a different feel than other RSU forks, whether that is worth other compromises is personal. I definitely take my Smashpot 40 over the Podium, but you want a damper that can provide more support with a coil.
  • 10
Endo79 (Nov 19, 2025 at 6:57)
 @Comatosegi: true, easily done with a custom tune on a damper though. Cheap as well. Got mine done at slick and slide. Custom valved as well.
  • 48
Dougal-SC (Nov 19, 2025 at 12:12) (Below Threshold)
 @Endo79: springs are springs. There's no benefit to stacking up two unless you're intentionally running a dual stage or need the extra stroke.

In my experience few smashpot riders get near full travel so the hbo isn't used much. But when it is the extra rebound damping from the HBO becomes a problem.
  • 30
Comatosegi (Nov 19, 2025 at 13:24)
 @Dougal-SC: I might be in the minority but I use the HBO on my Smashpot V2 multiple times per a run. With the V2 Steve said it only adds about ~5lbs of force in rebound, I haven’t been able to perceive a rebound issue, other than a noticeable topout, probably because I run rebound faster.

Application is a 200lb rider, 40 GX2, 53lb/in, HSC -3 LSC -6. V10 in standard firmer rear.
  • 37
Dougal-SC (Nov 19, 2025 at 13:58) (Below Threshold)
 @Comatosegi: that's exactly it. Need to run rebound much faster than any other coil because of the extra damping in deep stroke and then you get top out.

Without the fast rebound the fork noses down over every jump.
  • 40
TEAM-ROBOT (Nov 19, 2025 at 14:06)
 @Dougal-SC: Just out of curiosity, how much sag are you running on your fork? You've mentioned the nose-down jumping behavior of the Smashpot with HBO, but my understanding is that the HBO doesn't even come into effect until 40mm of travel, but that seems really deep to me for you to be going that far into travel on every jump face. I'm sure I go to my last 25% of fork travel on some jumps (the weird ones), but certainly not all of them.
  • 10
MorganBH FL (Nov 19, 2025 at 14:13)
 @Dougal-SC: I'm running a 50lb spring on a 38 grip 2 with the damping almost shut, and I bottom out occassionally on bigger drops. I'm about 210-215 geared up. Haven't really noticed a problem on the rebound side with it roughly in the middle.
  • 55
Dougal-SC (Nov 19, 2025 at 15:33)
 @TEAM-ROBOT: I don't measure sag, it's completely useless because everyone tries to measure in a different riding position.

The HBO is active in compression the last 40mm but the plunger is working the whole time and pushing/pulling oil for much more of the stroke. 90% sure I'm not getting into the last 40mm on a normal jump face. Other brands use an orifice and cup/cone style HBO which only works the rebound for the same stroke as the compression over a much shorter stroke. They don't nose down.

Mezzer and Dorado are 20mm and don't affect rebound outside that. Fox used to have HBO in the inverted FIT dampers. I never actually rode one.

@MorganBH A stock GRIP2VVC with the HSC/LSC shut isn't much compression damping, but it comes in harsh on the sharp stuff.
  • 20
Comatosegi (Nov 19, 2025 at 16:39)
 @Dougal-SC: My rebound settings were the same to how I ran it under an air spring. Used my old settings as a baseline and haven’t had to change it. Although there could be tolerance differences between the two 40s.

V2 is run with lighter 5wt oil and revised shims, is it possible the effect is drastically reduced in V2?
  • 35
Dougal-SC (Nov 19, 2025 at 17:04)
 @Comatosegi: It will be improved in the V2, the thinner oil is a big part of that. But V2 customers are still finding the issue.
  • 20
englertracing (Nov 19, 2025 at 17:17)
 @MorganBH: grip 2 vvc?
Full closed is still just about no damping in the case of 200lbs of man riding aggressively.
  • 10
pokbud (Nov 20, 2025 at 11:55)
 @Dougal-SC:
On V1
I run two shims only 65ML of oil and and lighter oil. So yeah, trying to lessen the effects of the HBO but mostly make it deeper into travel With that, I like the rebound on deep single compressions like jumps and drops. It just seems to calm the bike down and make it way more smoother and controlled and said scenarios.
  • 49
Dougal-SC (Nov 20, 2025 at 12:18) (Below Threshold)
 @pokbud: Starving it of oil works. It also reduces the ramp-up from trapped air. I couldn't make the bike fly level without full open fork rebound and rear shock rebound a lot slower than ideal. This meant for choppy ground at speed the suspension was compromised hugely.

With rebound working properly you don't have a problem recovering from deep single compressions. You might have bigger setup issues.
  • 181
VorsprungSuspension (Nov 20, 2025 at 14:59)
 @Dougal-SC: www.pinkbike.com/photo/29047892 You've repeated some demonstrably false claims here. This is the effect of the Smashpot HBO on rebound... ie effectively nothing. You can clearly see the travel at which it engages/disengages, and that the force in rebound (the lower half of the loop) isn't changing at that point, it's pretty well dead consistent. This is a sine wave test, but triangle waves show the same thing. There is no damper or spring installed during this test. Fork alignment on the dyno isn't perfect though and as there's no damper there's also no oil in the damper side, hence the fairly significant friction. If you're "nosing down" on jumps, it's not because your fork rebound is slowing down deep in the travel, nor is the ramp up from the air-over-oil compression very significant (we don't run a ton of oil in there, like about half what a 36 open bath used to run), it's lower than the ramp up of ANY modern air spring.

Riding technique is always the first thing you should look at when jumping, because a good rider can safely jump a terrible/dangerous setup, but there is no setup so good that you can't eject yourself with sufficiently poor technique. After that it's spring rate. Rebound damping has far less effect on jumping than most people seem think.
  • 10
MorganBH FL (Nov 20, 2025 at 16:36)
 @englertracing: On mine, full closed starts to act weird and spikey, I have to leave it one click open for it to stay predictable.
  • 38
Dougal-SC (Nov 20, 2025 at 16:51) (Below Threshold)
 @VorsprungSuspension: Your photo is set to private.

This is real, not only experienced by myself but many others. Dropping the oil out of the smashpot fixes it. Running the same coil in the same fork with a different coil conversion also fixes it.
  • 101
VorsprungSuspension (Nov 20, 2025 at 19:45)
 @Dougal-SC: fixed the visibility, dyno says the same thing you said above: “some problems only exist on the internet”
  • 69
Dougal-SC (Nov 20, 2025 at 20:25)
 @VorsprungSuspension: No speed mentioned on that graph, but it's showing ~6kg of rebound force. That's significant enough to cause the problems. Maybe 2 of that is fork chassis drag which would leave 4kg extra rebound damping.

Strangely I only found this problem riding, not on the internet. Same setup with no oil in the HBO and no rebound problem. Same coil with a different compression rod system also no rebound problem.
  • 131
VorsprungSuspension (Nov 20, 2025 at 20:37)
 @Dougal-SC: yeah except that it’s a very consistent force, neither speed sensitive nor position sensitive (ie nothing to do with HBO like you originally claimed, nor damping of any form), which illustrates that it’s friction, which is consistent with the fact that completely removing the Smashpot doesn’t change it. You can keep claiming it’s a problem all you want, but the most charitable interpretation of your claim is that you’re misdiagnosing a setup issue.
  • 510
Dougal-SC (Nov 20, 2025 at 23:17) (Below Threshold)
 @VorsprungSuspension: friction doesn't taper to zero like your plot does. It's clearly hydraulic and fits the problem perfectly.

That style of constant force damping gives very big damping ratios as speeds drop. The exact kind of thing that can cause nose drop in a fork.

If you're heavily sprung for park riding you might not notice. If you're sprung softer for spiky terrain you will.

You know it's not just me right? Other owners say they have raised this with vorsprung.
  • 112
VorsprungSuspension (Nov 21, 2025 at 10:33)
 @Dougal-SC: Once again, provably false claims from Dougal. www.pinkbike.com/photo/29050629

Green is with the Smashpot, blue is with the Smashpot disconnected, same fork, same dyno mount, no damper. As you can see, no increase in rebound damping due to the Smashpot. Please stop spreading misinformation here and on MTBR, the only other rider I've heard this from literally cited you as a source, so the common denominator and the source of this problem that does indeed only exist on the internet is pretty clear here.
  • 58
Dougal-SC (Nov 21, 2025 at 12:03)
 @VorsprungSuspension: treating customer feedback as misinformation is a really bizarre approach. Kinda ironic it's happening in push thread.

This is real and it's a big effect. Your graph of 6/10kg drag in a fork is an unrideable amount of drag. That's 2-3 times normal fork on a dyno. Suggest you check your data. Especially around the speeds that impact chassis movements.

You know how the whole aftermarket thinks air springs are poppy and coil is dead feeling? While actual Pogo sticks use coil springs because they have a stronger rebound? That's a smashpot phenomenon.
  • 91
VorsprungSuspension (Nov 21, 2025 at 12:33)
 @Dougal-SC: like I said, the fork alignment in the dyno on this isn't great for long and boring reasons (display fork, uncut steerer means it's so long we have to use a crappy old welded mount to have enough clearance to test), but the fact remains that these are two directly comparable tests where the only change is disconnecting the Smashpot and there is zero difference in the rebound. You can keep insisting that something is the case even when unambiguously proven wrong with hard data though, but that's what is truly "a really bizarre approach".

We take customer feedback very seriously, and we examine what they say and see if it matches measured reality. If someone's having problems with their bike nosing down on jumps, that may well be to do with the suspension, or it may well be technique, but in this case we've clearly proven it's not due to Dougal's Internet Problems. No doubt you'll keep arguing endlessly because that seems to be all you do, here's your shovel.
  • 38
Dougal-SC (Nov 21, 2025 at 17:26) (Below Threshold)
 @VorsprungSuspension:

So really believe these problems are due to rider technique? Yet experienced by multiple riders. They disappear when the oil is drained from the hbo. The rebound all returns to normal.....

The rebound also returns to normal with the same oil level, same springs, same bike, same rider but the HBO physically disabled.....

Back to back testing says no it's not technique or setup. My Dyno tests say no. Multiple riders all found the same issue. Had to run fork rebound full fast to compensate and keep the bike flying level. Where they didn't with any other coil or air spring.

Manufacturer says it's just rider technique. Posts up dyno charts with 3x more drag than ever seen before. Blames the riders technique and setup. Claims misinformation.

Case closed.

www.instagram.com/shockcraftsuspension/p/C2yCnjBB3qV
  • 72
VorsprungSuspension (Nov 21, 2025 at 17:43)
 @Dougal-SC: the funniest part is that you also posted a graph showing no change in rebound force from anything to do with HBO. Dig up…
  • 37
Dougal-SC (Nov 21, 2025 at 18:17) (Below Threshold)
 @VorsprungSuspension:

The HBO is active in all those plots. The additional rebound drag is consistent regardless of HBO valving.

For all those not familiar with dyno plots all the lines below the zero line are rebound. To have no rebound damping the lines would be along zero instead of below it.
  • 73
farkinoath (Nov 21, 2025 at 18:58)
 @Dougal-SC: seems you're the only one in this thread struggling to read a dyno plot but thanks for the 'pro' tip.
  • 52
VorsprungSuspension (Nov 21, 2025 at 19:10)
 @Dougal-SC: if the HBO had any contribution to rebound damping, you’d see a change in the rebound at the point in the travel where it disengages. But you don’t. Please, keep digging.
  • 37
Dougal-SC (Nov 21, 2025 at 20:22) (Below Threshold)
 The smashpot plunger is active for the whole stroke. Not just the HBO. You know this. The rod and plunger run up and down in a tube with oil with small clearance and limited oil exits top and bottom. It's literally a dashpot damper. Just like your logo. This works for the entire stroke. It pulls oil up with every decent stroke. The stronger HBO effect only happens when the extended plunger tip enters the smaller HBO chamber with the shims and port adjuster to control pressure and exit flow. I even tried to fix the rebound damping issue on the v1 smashpot with modifications to the hbo refill ports but they didn't help. Because the problem is above the HBO circuit. That mod did prove the location of the extra damping though.
  • 102
VorsprungSuspension (Nov 21, 2025 at 23:04)
 @Dougal-SC: this gets funnier and funnier. That’d be great other than the whole plunger moving through air not oil thing.

You see “rebound damping” in any test with any open bath unit (Smashpot or other damper) because you inherently have to test it inside a fork chassis, which by virtue of its oil lubricated nature and sliding seals, will always generate some resistance to motion that has a combination of both viscous (speed sensitive) and non viscous resistance. This is not an opinion, it is clearly visible in the second plot I posted previously, which has no damper and no Smashpot.

It’s pretty amusing how before you were saying “the hbo adds rebound damping”, now it’s “the Smashpot adds rebound damping”, and no matter how much hard data proves that you do not know what you’re talking about, you’re absolutely determined to die on this hill. Your mods didn’t “work” because you do not sufficiently understand how the fork works as a system. That isn’t a Smashpot issue, it’s a Dougal issue.
  • 55
Dougal-SC (Nov 22, 2025 at 0:32)
 @VorsprungSuspension:

Here's a pic of your problem:

www.pinkbike.com/photo/29052685

Zoomed in here: www.pinkbike.com/photo/29052686

Give the ad hominem a rest. You're better than that.
  • 50
rm7freerider (Nov 24, 2025 at 21:15)
 I have the Smashpot V2 and even with HBO turned all the way out it nose-dives after big compressions, like g-out to jump. That affects the bike attitude and rotates the front wheel down/raises the rear. I find this dangerous. I'm trying to find a way to solve this, but so far no dice.
  • 50
VorsprungSuspension (Nov 26, 2025 at 14:22)
 @rm7freerider: from your location I'm guessing you're James/Jayem who I've been emailing with? Let me know how you go with the increased oil height/progression, as I suspect the issue you're getting there is that the spring forces deep in the travel at the front are lower than you want them to be. Could be as simple as wanting a firmer spring rate too.

@Dougal-SC - very slow reply as I've been away, but you're right, that was an unnecessary dig at you, and I apologise. With your second pic, now we're getting somewhere. There are two main differences between what you've tested and what I've tested and presented here:
1. The dyno plots you have are for a Smashpot V1, with what I would assume is also 20wt oil in there, whereas what I've posted up are for the V2, with the specified 5wt oil in there.
2. What I did is remove the Smashpot connection (no topcap installed), but left the oil in there, and as we can see, there's no change in rebound there - indicating that (at least with the V2 as tested) there is no measurable amount of . You left the Smashpot installed, but removed (what I would assume is) the 20wt oil. That would initially suggest that it is in fact the difference in the dynamic drag of the bushings with different amounts of oil that you are measuring. That is supported by the fact that there isn't really any mechanism by which the Smashpot can push oil through the sealhead, since the oil level isn't high enough to fill the cartridge like that - it's pushing air around up there, and there is a ton of flow area for that. The V1 cartridge had a lot of clearance between the plunger and the tube as well.

Third, your initial claim was that the HBO is responsible for increased rebound damping. We can see from your posted curves AND mine, that that isn't the case. You do show an increase in rebound damping overall in your dyno tests between oil installed/removed, but if it were to do with the HBO, you'd see a substantial change when it disengages, which we don't, in your tests or mine.

I'm also not entirely clear on the parameters with which you ran your dyno tests, such as whether gas spring force was removed (doesn't look like it was, but could be wrong), test velocity etc, but if you want to provide those, I'll find a V1 kit and replicate them here with just the Smashpot removed and the oil remaining.
  • 41
Dougal-SC (Nov 26, 2025 at 16:12)
 @VorsprungSuspension: appreciate the apology. We should be able to discuss technical problems without insults.

The extra damping is absolutely not bushing drag. Wrong profile. It's hydraulic damping from the plunger in aerated oil which softens the expected engagement points. It will be worth you doing some more in depth testing on this.

The oil was 68k which was both heated to lower viscosity by the repetitive Dyno testing and also foamed/aerated. So significantly less drag than 20wt.

@rm7freerider is a different name and location to Jayem. This extra damping problem has affected my customers all over the globe. If there was a simple solution we'd be all over it. I went back to air .
  • 40
VorsprungSuspension (Nov 27, 2025 at 11:46)
 @Dougal-SC: I think RM7freerider is in fact located in Alaska by his pics, and his profile has pics that include very specific criticisms Jayem has made of, for example, old hydracoil Boxxers (yes it's weird that I remember these things from 20 years ago), so I think it is indeed Jayem.

What velocity was your testing run at? As I said, happy to replicate that with a V1.

The V2, as shown, generates no significant additional damping, or removing the topcap would show up some discrepancy between the curves. The fork wasn't even taken off the dyno to make that change, I just pulled the topcap out of the crown and off the cartridge, and let the cartridge sit in the bottom. There may well be setup difficulties that people have with their fork with a Smashpot installed, but at this point in time we can definitively see that if they're running a V2 kit it's not an "extra damping" problem from the Smashpot. I would suggest that if anything, a higher rate of progression at the rear of the bike than the front in many cases might mean that more spring progression is beneficial, meaning a HIGHER oil volume in the fork not a lower one. James has communicated to me that his main point of comparison was a Zeb with a Secus, which has a much more progressive end stroke than does the Smashpot.
  • 11
rm7freerider (Nov 29, 2025 at 21:30)
 @VorsprungSuspension: No no, I ride the most perfect bike ever made, the RM7! It was only designed to self-destruct from inception. And yes, that's who I am. I do not think a stiffer spring rate is the answer, if anything, I'm on the side of oversprung vs. under. I'm not able to test next week, my trip down south was shortened...but I have the fork before-Smashpot to compare to and other forks and just none of my other setups did this and I'm well aware of what being under-sprung does. The Smashpot sucks down on compressions for me and as I said before, I had to take it off the bike for safety reasons. I'll do some more testing when I can and do have some opportunities in the winter for it, but I've never had a fork suck down like this. I had to purposefully under-spring the rear end of the bike to get some semblance of balance, didn't correct the issue, but more symptomatic that it was an untenable situation. I do want it to work, I do appreciate coil spring. I think the Mezzer air spring suffers from not having a big enough negative spring for 180mm of travel due to using spacers instead of air shafts and that plus the seal-drag seems to make it beat my hands when set up linear for harder riding, vs. diving all over the place if set up progressive. I tend to think most riders set it up progressive and just go slow...which is fine, but I want it to hold up during harder riding and also not beat up my hands. Somehow the Zeb with the Secus does this, but even the old Lyrik with the Push ACS3 and Avalanche cartridge was far better than the coil Mezzer. Again, no suck-down.
  • 30
Dougal-SC (Nov 30, 2025 at 13:11)
 @VorsprungSuspension: Those tests were CVP at 1m/s peak. Velocity into the HBO spike is about 0.7m/s.

I haven't tested the G2. I have yet to see one.

More spring and/or progression will increase the rebound speed, but it's a workaround that impacts every other aspect of the suspension setup. Including front/rear balance.

One other issue was the damping from the smashpot being variable depending on how hard the fork is worked and how much the oil inside is foamed. Off the dyno it's all foam. Typical of open bath.
  • 20
ultimatist (Nov 18, 2025 at 10:09)

Kilian1b15Gt (Nov 19, 2025 at 2:12)
 Since when are 100Nm/750W mid power? Or 111Nm/720W?





Dont the following too seriously, light hearted intentions and such:


Fox Podium releases
Intend fanboys: "our forks are stiff enough in every regard, no need for that hefty crown or 20mm axle"
Also intend forks: twist a solid 30° when trying to turn a twisted stem back without losening the stem bolts

Fox: the Podium matches the torsional stiffness of a 36
Reviewers: there is a bit more torsional vagueness in the Podium than on a 36/38 when braking and pushing through corners
PB comments after Push USD review: apparently the 20mm axle makes sense, Fox wasnt telling us BS and the bike industry isnt trying to sell us new hubs just because

englertracing (Nov 18, 2025 at 20:08)

Lagr1980 FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 21:47)
 @englertracing: yeah, that's an opinion... but some people think of it as a compromise.. and use case... no-compromise builds for adults & racing.. yeah go with the most solid setup.... we are fll of snobbery in MTB , like in everything.... englertracing (Nov 18, 2025 at 22:49)
 @Lagr1980: it's my actual opinion that dropping 20mm axles from 36 and up forks was a poor choice. Has that not happened, your trail bike, enduro, and dh bike would likely be sharing wheels, also lots of 20mm hubs used to take 15mm end caps anyways.... for 200lbs agressive riders a 20mm axle is (or should be) desirable.

Kilian1b15Gt (Nov 19, 2025 at 2:31)
 @ratedgg13: Cannondale Lefty also runs on guide rails and not on bushings that allow a rotation.
  • 30
nicoenduro (Nov 19, 2025 at 10:25)
 @Dougal-SC: when asked Intend also told me, if you torque the pinch bolts on the 15mm axle more it's going to be as stiff as the 20mm, there's a 4% difference between the 2
  • 11
garrettstories (Nov 20, 2025 at 8:10)
 @englertracing: I do love rocks! True that Marzocchi made a single crown USD fork long ago, and some overseas players have made a few more recently, but I was thinking how in this travel range and INTENDed use, they beat Fox to the punch. Maybe my comment was too US centric.
  • 10
rm7freerider (Nov 25, 2025 at 21:32)
 @ratedgg13: Lefty forks are not a valid comparison, they have what are essentially "square" stanchions, so there is no torsional flex (like we see on forks with round stanchions). This makes them exceptionally stiff, probably not nearly as dependent on the axle, although a tapered axle is the best way to distribute the load.
  • 10
ratedgg13 FL (Nov 26, 2025 at 6:19)
 @rm7freerider: then why has nobody tried square stanchions for both sides of their USD fork?
  • 10
Dougal-SC (Nov 26, 2025 at 8:21)
 @ratedgg13: Cannondale did that years ago. The Moto DH fork. They basically started with half that for the lefty concept.

Lefty forks have several flex modes that dual sided forks do not have. That's why no-one takes them seriously.
  • 10
ratedgg13 FL (Nov 26, 2025 at 10:05)
 @Dougal-SC: okay, but that's years ago. Seems to me like there's tons of innovations brands COULD be using to solve these problems, but arent
  • 20
Dougal-SC (Nov 26, 2025 at 13:30)
 @ratedgg13: That's because those "innovations" didn't help. The forks were heavy, clunky and couldn't keep oil in.
Other brands have tried "keyed" stanchions on USD forks. It increases friction significantly. The most successful way to help any fork is bigger axle, crown and fork brace. The USD obviously has no brace so leans more heavily on axle and crown.
  • 64
Blownoutrides (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:44)

Hard pressed to believe these aren't preconceived "findings".
  • 64
loam2home FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 8:39)
 Great honest review on this.... sticking with my Zeb.
  • 21
LDG FL (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:04)
 @mikekazimer I've seen you say you use 82-83psi in the Fox 38 Grip X2 but how do you do you set up rebound and compression?
  • 1921
CCalomino (Nov 18, 2025 at 9:16)
 Lol Fox advertisement money at work lol.

Podium is stiff but deflects like a mofo mid corner bumps and if not at Mach chicken speed its no better than a good tuned 38. I've tested both on same bike.

I've run the 9.1 v2 fork for a few months on same bike as other 2 forks and its nihht and day difference. Traction and feel alone. The enduro lever is impressive on nasty tracks when extra support is needed. 90% of riders will love the feel and forgiveness ,confidence of the 9.1.

Podium needs and new front hub, new air shaft to change travel, and disassemble for service.

9.1 you can change oil on bike fully assembled. Regular boost spacing, quick easy travel adjustments with no needed extra parts.

Push does a good job of letting you upgrade aswell as new development to their products comes available.

Is it cheap? No but what is in the bike industry these days. I've never scene someone jump on a bike with this fork set up and ever want to go back to previous set up. I dare you to compare yourself and then laugh at this review.
  • 198
mikekazimer FL Editor (Nov 18, 2025 at 10:44)
 None of our reviews are paid for - we get the products in, test them, and then report our findings. Simple as that. I don't think people will laugh at this review, but like I said at the end of the video, I'd love for more people to be able to try this fork. I'd be curious what other riders (ideally ones who didn't just cough up $2,250 for a new fork) thought about it.
  • 63
phops (Nov 18, 2025 at 12:06)
 >I've never scene someone jump on a bike with this fork set up and ever want to go back to previous set up.

I would absolutely do just that, because of the weight. For trail bikes (which includes enduro), I like my front end light and poppy.

For fast DH riding, I prefer to be on 200mm dual crown. There is no replacement for having more travel. I used to have a Canfield One.2, now I am on a Starling Dive, 160/180, carbon rims, smaller cassette in the back (due to smaller diameter wheels not needing big cogs), and with a older RS Boxxer with 35mm stanchions, it weights like 36 lbs.
  • 148
CCalomino (Nov 18, 2025 at 12:23)
 @mikekazimer: I tested all 3 forks on a PNW Relay size XL and im a 195lbs 6ft 3 rider for reference.

When the 9.1 came out my first thought was lol $2200 ya nope this is crazy. My tuned 38 is just as good. Then I got to test one out on familiar trails. More traction, less hand fatigue and composure that left me in a state shock. Month later I tried out a podium and it was crisp on turn in but low speed it deflects alot. Wide open its comes to life more. 90% of your audience does mot spend there time at KOM knife edge speed all day.

My money went where the performance was. I dont need to validate my purchase. Hell im Probably gonna put one on my anolog bike aswell. The weight penalty is well worth the DH performance.

How is the 9.1 so expensive compared to the podium when I gotta buy a new hub and rebuild or buy a complete specific wheel for it? The QC between the 2 is light years apart. The podium has stiction issues and an air valve that loves to puke oil on the brake rotor.The first 200 hours of maintenance on a 9.1 can be done by a 5 year old its so easy. Zero shop down time / cost for those 4 services minus the fluid kit.

The fact you and Dario gotta get in the comments and discredit your audience's comments to the review cracks me up. If you were so secure in your review you would let it stand vs fight to prove its truth. The industry is in turmoil and the outside money is drying up so dont rock the boat with the big dogs we get it and you have made it very apparent.

Call me a hack,shil or a nobody honestly I dont care. What i know is this. If you have the money to spend on an inverted fork go try both for yourself and you will see the performance difference pretty quick. Then look at the overall cost of ownership to get up and running and the versatility for future swap to your next bike and it makes even more sense.
  • 123
pmhobson (Nov 18, 2025 at 12:31)
 @CCalomino: In what way did MK "discredit" your comment? He refuted an accusation, but he didn't tell you that your experience or opinion was "wrong".
  • 97
CCalomino (Nov 18, 2025 at 12:54)
 @pmhobson: Mike stated he wants to hear from those that have not forked out the $2250 for the fork already. Implying since im an owner my opinion is void or bias.
  • 143
mikekazimer FL Editor (Nov 18, 2025 at 13:03)
 @CCalomino, the comment section is what makes Pinkbike great. I'm confident in my review of this fork, and yes, I could just sit back and stay out of the comments, but I like being able to interact with readers / viewers and help clarify points or answer questions.
  • 141
pmhobson (Nov 18, 2025 at 13:07)
 @CCalomino: If you think that shelling out $2,000 *doesn't* make us consumers subject to confirmation and reinforcement bias, then I don't know what to tell you.

  • 1121
dododuzzi (Nov 19, 2025 at 8:51)
 The occasional HATCHET JOB by Mike Kazimer! He either gets a kick doing this, indulging his personal biases from the pinkbike pulpit, or it is a necessary ingredient to give some credibility to an ocean of "tests" praising anything that enters the market.

This particular hatchet job is so bad, ignoring the literally hundreds of glowing reviews appearing on the MTBR forum (or the PUSH website), that is not even worth going through the list. Stands out? What you get for the price: it does not even mention that the price includes a custom set up out of the factory. And the weight ... it is a COIL fork, of course it weights two hundred grams more than a Podium.

And really ... taking it around a ultra-smooth trail without seemingly ever leaving the ground is a "test ride"? Bravo!
  • 74
ljr20 FL (Nov 20, 2025 at 4:00)
 Why anyone takes buying advice from these Pinkbike reviews is a mystery. Dario can't even bring himself to ride the manufacturer recommended size frames. Kazimer is so obsessed by weight, why he was the one to review these forks is a mystery, especially as Dario reviewed the Podium.

Matt Beer does a good job but the days of Dan Robert's reviews and knowledge are long gone and we are left with slop and "thought experiment lol" articles and data collecting polls.
  • 23
mikekazimer FL Editor (Nov 20, 2025 at 9:07)
 @ljr20, I'm definitely not obsessed with weight, but 3,000 grams is very heavy for a fork, enough that it's noticeable while riding.

@dododuzzi, nice of you to chime in as always. I wouldn't be doing my job if I just parroted other reviews - this review is based on my actual testing, which included (as I mentioned) lots of laps in the Whistler bike park - those trails definitely aren't smooth in the middle of the summer.
  • 43
dododuzzi (Nov 22, 2025 at 7:17)
 @mikekazimer: there you go again ... now it is 3000 grams. The weight is 200 grams more than the Podium, which is an air fork. Are you telling us that the weight of the Podium is not noticeable, but the Push weight is? Fox juggernaut vs Push? And Fox is ONLY $200 less? When Push is delivered with a custom tuning?

Look at the result, read the comments on this thread. This is a social-media-style hatchet job, something to really be ashamed of.
  • 12
mikekazimer FL Editor (Nov 24, 2025 at 11:26)
 @dododuzzi, the Podium's heavy too, and as I said in the review, I'd prefer the 38 over either one if it was my money.
